card.title: The Ancient Art of Bonsai
card.teaser: It's not a miniature species — it's a full-size tree convinced to live its whole life in a teacup.
card.coverImagePrompt: A weathered 200-year-old Japanese black pine bonsai on a dark wooden stand, soft window light revealing every needle and gnarled branch, tokonoma alcove setting, cinematic shallow depth of field

lessons.title: A 400-year-old tree in a pot
lessons.body: In a Tokyo nursery sits a white pine called the 'Sandai Shogun-no-Matsu' — planted around 1610 and cared for continuously ever since. It's only a meter tall. That's not a dwarf tree. It's a regular Japanese white pine that has been living in a shallow ceramic tray for four centuries, passed from gardener to gardener like a relay baton.

visual.imagePrompt: An ancient Japanese white pine bonsai with a thick gnarled trunk in a blue ceramic tray, placed in the Imperial Palace garden, soft golden light

lessons.title: Not a species — a practice
lessons.body: The word bonsai (盆栽) literally means 'tray planting.' Almost any tree can become one: pines, maples, junipers, even apples or pomegranates. What makes it a bonsai isn't genetics — it's the ongoing practice of root pruning, branch wiring, and pot confinement that keeps a normal tree in a miniature form.

keyTerms.definition: Japanese for 'tray planting' — the art of growing full-species trees in confined containers
Wrapping branches in copper or aluminum wire to bend them into shape over months

visual.imagePrompt: Close-up of hands using specialized bonsai scissors to trim a juniper, copper wire wrapped around a branch

lessons.title: How you shrink a tree
lessons.body: Two techniques do most of the work. Root pruning every 2–5 years cuts back the tree's underground mass, which limits how big the above-ground part can grow. Branch wiring lets you bend trunks and limbs into graceful curves that mimic the shapes of ancient wind-blown mountain trees. Combine them and you get a tree with the proportions of an old giant — scaled down a hundredfold.

keyTerms.definition: Trimming a tree's roots to control its overall size and force dense, fine new growth

visual.imagePrompt: Illustrated diagram showing before/after of a bonsai being root-pruned, fine roots exposed against soil, followed by trunk wiring

lessons.title: Imported from China, refined in Japan
lessons.body: The practice began in China more than 1,300 years ago as 'penjing' — landscape scenes in trays — and was brought to Japan by Buddhist monks around the 6th century. The Japanese stripped it down to focus on a single tree as the centerpiece. By the 14th century, bonsai was an aristocratic pursuit; today, world-class specimens sell for over one million dollars at auction.
lessons.funFact: A Japanese white pine that survived the Hiroshima atomic bomb in 1945 is now on display at the U.S. National Arboretum — donated by the family that tended it for 300 years.

visual.imagePrompt: Historical scroll painting of a Chinese penjing landscape in a tray, transitioning to a Japanese zen garden with a single bonsai

lessons.title: A conversation across generations
lessons.body: A serious bonsai outlives its caretaker. The tree you buy and train for 30 years might be passed to your grandchildren, then to theirs. The art isn't really about controlling a tree — it's about entering a slow conversation with one that will speak back long after you're gone.

quiz.question: What does the word 'bonsai' literally mean?
quiz.explanation: 'Bon' means tray or shallow pot, and 'sai' means planting — so bonsai literally means tray planting.

quiz.question: What keeps a bonsai small?
quiz.explanation: Bonsai are regular tree species kept small through ongoing root pruning, wiring, and pot confinement.

quiz.question: Where did the practice of bonsai originate?
quiz.explanation: The art originated in China as 'penjing' over 1,300 years ago before being brought to Japan by Buddhist monks.
